continuity.sourceBoundary: My Hero Academia manga chapters 1-430. Chapter 431 'More' is treated as an optional post-series extra overlay unless explicitly loaded.
continuity.notes: Anime-only scenes, movies, OVAs, Vigilantes, Team-Up Missions, light novels, games, and filler material are excluded from the primary deck family unless added as separate overlay decks.

content.fact: U.A. High is an elite hero school whose training exposes students to high stakes earlier than ordinary education would.
content.injection: Write U.A. as prestigious, competitive, and risky; its promise of training does not make it a safe bubble.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

entries.title: Class 1-A Is A Mixed Talent Cohort

continuity.sourceBoundary: My Hero Academia manga chapters 1-430. Chapter 431 'More' is treated as an optional post-series extra overlay unless explicitly loaded.
continuity.notes: Anime-only scenes, movies, OVAs, Vigilantes, Team-Up Missions, light novels, games, and filler material are excluded from the primary deck family unless added as separate overlay decks.

content.fact: Class 1-A combines prodigies, disciplined students, comic personalities, anxious beginners, and unconventional Quirks.
content.injection: Class 1-A should feel like a varied cohort, not a uniform squad of equally mature combat specialists.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

content.fact: Aizawa uses harsh tests, blunt warnings, and controlled pressure to force students to confront hero work realistically.
content.injection: Aizawa should sound severe and practical; his pressure is meant to expose risk and weakness, not to provide warm encouragement first.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

content.fact: All Might is a legendary hero but initially lacks polished classroom teaching instincts.
content.injection: Early All Might teaching scenes should show charisma and hero wisdom mixed with awkward planning and secrecy.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.

content.fact: Attacks and media intrusion force U.A. to tighten security and treat the school as a target.
content.injection: After villain contact, U.A. security should become an active concern rather than background decoration.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.

content.fact: Hero Course students know they are being measured against peers, pro expectations, and their own public ambitions.
content.injection: Student scenes should preserve competition, insecurity, rivalry, and growth pressure without making classmates purely hostile.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

content.fact: U.A. exercises often test judgment, cooperation, rescue sense, and self-control beyond the stated objective.
content.injection: Do not treat U.A. exercises as simple win-loss games; teachers may be measuring qualities students do not notice yet.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

content.fact: U.A. pushes students to develop hero names, costumes, teamwork, interviews, and public-facing self-presentation.
content.injection: School scenes can include identity formation because hero work requires public presentation as well as power.

entries.title: Dorms Are Not Present At Story Start

content.fact: Class 1-A does not begin the story living in dorms; dorm life is a later safety response after major villain escalation.
content.injection: Before the dorm move, do not place Class 1-A in U.A. dorms.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
antiLore: Do not write U.A. as a consequence-free superhero clubhouse.

content.fact: U.A. faculty must continue education while responding to direct threats against students and the institution.
content.injection: After attacks, faculty scenes should carry both school-management and hero-crisis responsibilities.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.

content.injection: Early Class 1-A scenes should allow unfamiliarity at first, then sharper trust after shared crises.

content.injection: When a U.A. event is public, make reputation, scouting, audience reaction, and future internships relevant.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.

content.fact: Early Class 1-A students may be brave and talented but often lack field judgment, emotional control, and experience.
content.injection: Before major training and internships, students should make plausible beginner mistakes under pressure.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.

content.fact: Teachers may withhold full context to manage student safety, morale, school reputation, or hero operations.
content.injection: Faculty must not explain every institutional secret to students immediately, especially after villain events.
constraints: Keep student age, supervision, school routine, and institutional risk visible when U.A. is the scene frame.
